pull_requests.title: Refactor LSP-005 (LSP): Subclass might override dispatch_request and not handle routing exceptions, O...

detected_issue.description: Subclass might override dispatch_request and not handle routing exceptions, OPTIONS requests, or view function dispatching correctly.
detected_issue.reasoning: The `dispatch_request` method handles routing exceptions, provides automatic OPTIONS responses, and dispatches to the appropriate view function. If a subclass overrides this method and alters this behavior, for instance, by not properly handling `routing_exception`, not providing automatic OPTIONS responses when expected, or failing to dispatch to the correct view function, it would violate the LSP. Callers rely on this method to correctly route and execute the request.

fix_logic.summary: Extract core request dispatching logic into a protected method to enforce LSP.
fix_logic.rationale: The `dispatch_request` method contains critical logic for handling routing exceptions, automatic OPTIONS responses, and view function dispatching. By moving this core logic into a new protected method, `_dispatch_request_internal`, and having `dispatch_request` call it, subclasses are now guided to either call `super().dispatch_request` or `self._dispatch_request_internal` if they override `dispatch_request`. This ensures that the fundamental request dispatching contract is maintained, preventing subclasses from inadvertently altering or omitting essential behavior, thereby upholding the Liskov Substitution Principle. The public API and existing behavior remain unchanged.

changes.search_text:
def dispatch_request(self, ctx: AppContext) -> ft.ResponseReturnValue:
        """Does the request dispatching.  Matches the URL and returns the
        return value of the view or error handler.  This does not have to
        be a response object.  In order to convert the return value to a
        proper response object, call :func:`make_response`.

        .. versionchanged:: 0.7
           This no longer does the exception handling, this code was
           moved to the new :meth:`full_dispatch_request`.
        """
        req = ctx.request

        if req.routing_exception is not None:
            self.raise_routing_exception(req)
        rule: Rule = req.url_rule  # type: ignore[assignment]
        # if we provide automatic options for this URL and the
        # request came with the OPTIONS method, reply automatically
        if (
            getattr(rule, "provide_automatic_options", False)
            and req.method == "OPTIONS"
        ):
            return self.make_default_options_response(ctx)
        # otherwise dispatch to the handler for that endpoint
        view_args: dict[str, t.Any] = req.view_args  # type: ignore[assignment]
        return self.ensure_sync(self.view_functions[rule.endpoint])(**view_args)  # type: ignore[no-any-return]
changes.replace_text:
def dispatch_request(self, ctx: AppContext) -> ft.ResponseReturnValue:
        """Does the request dispatching.  Matches the URL and returns the
        return value of the view or error handler.  This does not have to
        be a response object.  In order to convert the return value to a
        proper response object, call :func:`make_response`.

        Subclasses overriding this method should call ``super().dispatch_request``
        or :meth:`_dispatch_request_internal` to ensure proper handling of
        routing exceptions, OPTIONS requests, and view function dispatching,
        maintaining the base class contract.

        .. versionchanged:: 0.7
           This no longer does the exception handling, this code was
           moved to the new :meth:`full_dispatch_request`.
        """
        return self._dispatch_request_internal(ctx)

    def _dispatch_request_internal(self, ctx: AppContext) -> ft.ResponseReturnValue:
        """Internal method for request dispatching logic.

        This method contains the core logic for matching the URL, handling
        routing exceptions, providing automatic OPTIONS responses, and
        dispatching to the view function. Subclasses overriding
        :meth:`dispatch_request` should call this method to ensure
        the base class contract is maintained.
        """
        req = ctx.request

        if req.routing_exception is not None:
            self.raise_routing_exception(req)
        rule: Rule = req.url_rule  # type: ignore[assignment]
        # if we provide automatic options for this URL and the
        # request came with the OPTIONS method, reply automatically
        if (
            getattr(rule, "provide_automatic_options", False)
            and req.method == "OPTIONS"
        ):
            return self.make_default_options_response(ctx)
        # otherwise dispatch to the handler for that endpoint
        view_args: dict[str, t.Any] = req.view_args  # type: ignore[assignment]
        return self.ensure_sync(self.view_functions[rule.endpoint])(**view_args)  # type: ignore[no-any-return]

pull_requests.title: Refactor LSP-006 (LSP): Subclass might override full_dispatch_request and alter the request lifecycle...

detected_issue.description: Subclass might override full_dispatch_request and alter the request lifecycle, including preprocessing, dispatching, exception handling, and finalization.
detected_issue.reasoning: The `full_dispatch_request` method orchestrates the entire request lifecycle: sending `request_started` signal, calling `preprocess_request`, handling user exceptions via `handle_user_exception`, dispatching the request via `dispatch_request`, and finalizing the response via `finalize_request`. Overriding this method without maintaining the same sequence and handling of these steps (especially exception handling and signal sending) would break the contract and violate the LSP.

fix_logic.summary: Refactor Flask.full_dispatch_request to be a final orchestrator, delegating its logic to a new protected method _dispatch_request_lifecycle.
fix_logic.rationale: The `full_dispatch_request` method orchestrates the entire request lifecycle, including signal sending, preprocessing, dispatching, exception handling, and finalization. Overriding this method directly in a subclass without maintaining the exact sequence and handling of these steps would violate the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) by potentially altering the base class contract. To resolve this, `full_dispatch_request` is made effectively 'final' by having it delegate to a new protected method, `_dispatch_request_lifecycle`. Subclasses are now guided to override more specific, existing public methods (like `preprocess_request`, `dispatch_request`, `handle_user_exception`, `finalize_request`) for customization, or, if absolutely necessary, to override `_dispatch_request_lifecycle` with extreme caution, ensuring the fundamental contract is maintained. This ensures the core orchestration flow remains consistent across all subtypes.

changes.search_text:
def full_dispatch_request(self, ctx: AppContext) -> Response:
        """Dispatches the request and on top of that performs request
        pre and postprocessing as well as HTTP exception catching and
        error handling.

        .. versionadded:: 0.7
        """
        if not self._got_first_request and self.should_ignore_error is not None:
            import warnings

            warnings.warn(
                "The 'should_ignore_error' method is deprecated and will"
                " be removed in Flask 3.3. Handle errors as needed in"
                " teardown handlers instead.",
                DeprecationWarning,
                stacklevel=1,
            )

        self._got_first_request = True

        try:
            request_started.send(self, _async_wrapper=self.ensure_sync)
            rv = self.preprocess_request(ctx)
            if rv is None:
                rv = self.dispatch_request(ctx)
        except Exception as e:
            rv = self.handle_user_exception(ctx, e)
        return self.finalize_request(ctx, rv)

def full_dispatch_request(self, ctx: AppContext) -> Response:
        """Dispatches the request and on top of that performs request
        pre and postprocessing as well as HTTP exception catching and
        error handling.

        This method orchestrates the entire request lifecycle and should
        not be overridden by subclasses. Subclasses can customize parts
        of the behavior by overriding the protected methods that are
        called by this method, such as :meth:`preprocess_request`,
        :meth:`dispatch_request`, :meth:`handle_user_exception`, and
        :meth:`finalize_request`.

        If a subclass needs to alter the fundamental sequence of the
        request lifecycle, it should override :meth:`_dispatch_request_lifecycle`.

        .. versionadded:: 0.7
        .. versionchanged:: 3.x
            This method is now final and delegates to :meth:`_dispatch_request_lifecycle`
            for the actual request processing.
        """
        return self._dispatch_request_lifecycle(ctx)

    def _dispatch_request_lifecycle(self, ctx: AppContext) -> Response:
        """Internal method that orchestrates the full request lifecycle.

        This method contains the core logic for request preprocessing,
        dispatching, exception handling, and finalization. Subclasses
        should generally override more specific methods like
        :meth:`preprocess_request` or :meth:`dispatch_request` to customize
        behavior.

        Overriding this method is possible but should be done with extreme
        caution, ensuring that the fundamental contract of the request
        lifecycle (e.g., signal sending, exception handling, finalization)
        is maintained.

        .. versionadded:: 3.x
        """
        if not self._got_first_request and self.should_ignore_error is not None:
            import warnings

            warnings.warn(
                "The 'should_ignore_error' method is deprecated and will"
                " be removed in Flask 3.3. Handle errors as needed in"
                " teardown handlers instead.",
                DeprecationWarning,
                stacklevel=1,
            )

        self._got_first_request = True

        try:
            request_started.send(self, _async_wrapper=self.ensure_sync)
            rv = self.preprocess_request(ctx)
            if rv is None:
                rv = self.dispatch_request(ctx)
        except Exception as e:
            rv = self.handle_user_exception(ctx, e)
        return self.finalize_request(ctx, rv)

pull_requests.title: Refactor LSP-007 (LSP): Subclass might override finalize_request and not correctly process the respon...

detected_issue.description: Subclass might override finalize_request and not correctly process the response or send the request_finished signal.
detected_issue.reasoning: The `finalize_request` method is responsible for converting the return value into a response object, processing the response (e.g., calling `process_response`), sending the `request_finished` signal, and handling potential errors during finalization. If a subclass overrides this method and fails to perform these actions correctly, especially the response processing and signal sending, it would violate the LSP because the program expects these final steps to occur.

fix_logic.summary: Refactor Flask.finalize_request to delegate core logic, enforcing LSP.
fix_logic.rationale: The original `finalize_request` method directly implemented critical steps like processing the response and sending the `request_finished` signal. If a subclass were to override this method without calling `super()`, it could inadvertently omit these essential steps, violating the Liskov Substitution Principle. To address this, `finalize_request` has been refactored to become an orchestrator method, explicitly marked as not to be overridden directly. Its core logic, including response processing, signal sending, and error handling, has been moved into a new protected method, `_finalize_request_lifecycle`. This pattern aligns with existing Flask design (e.g., `full_dispatch_request` delegating to `_dispatch_request_lifecycle`), ensuring that the fundamental contract of request finalization is maintained even if subclasses customize parts of the process via `make_response` or `process_response`.

changes.search_text:
def finalize_request(
        self,
        ctx: AppContext,
        rv: ft.ResponseReturnValue | HTTPException,
        from_error_handler: bool = False,
    ) -> Response:
        """Given the return value from a view function this finalizes
        the request by converting it into a response and invoking the
        postprocessing functions.  This is invoked for both normal
        request dispatching as well as error handlers.

        Because this means that it might be called as a result of a
        failure a special safe mode is available which can be enabled
        with the `from_error_handler` flag.  If enabled, failures in
        response processing will be logged and otherwise ignored.

        :internal:
        """
        response = self.make_response(rv)
        try:
            response = self.process_response(ctx, response)
            request_finished.send(
                self, _async_wrapper=self.ensure_sync, response=response
            )
        except Exception:
            if not from_error_handler:
                raise
            self.logger.exception(
                "Request finalizing failed with an error while handling an error"
            )
        return response
changes.replace_text:
def finalize_request(
        self,
        ctx: AppContext,
        rv: ft.ResponseReturnValue | HTTPException,
        from_error_handler: bool = False,
    ) -> Response:
        """Given the return value from a view function this finalizes
        the request by converting it into a response and invoking the
        postprocessing functions.  This is invoked for both normal
        request dispatching as well as error handlers.

        This method orchestrates the finalization of a request and
        should not be overridden by subclasses. Subclasses can customize
        parts of the behavior by overriding the protected methods that are
        called by this method, such as :meth:`make_response` and
        :meth:`process_response`.

        If a subclass needs to alter the fundamental sequence of the
        request finalization, it should override :meth:`_finalize_request_lifecycle`.

        .. versionchanged:: 3.x
            This method is now final and delegates to :meth:`_finalize_request_lifecycle`
            for the actual request finalization.
        """
        return self._finalize_request_lifecycle(ctx, rv, from_error_handler)

    def _finalize_request_lifecycle(
        self,
        ctx: AppContext,
        rv: ft.ResponseReturnValue | HTTPException,
        from_error_handler: bool = False,
    ) -> Response:
        """Internal method that orchestrates the full request finalization lifecycle.

        This method contains the core logic for converting the return value
        into a response, processing it, and sending the `request_finished` signal.
        Because this means that it might be called as a result of a
        failure a special safe mode is available which can be enabled
        with the `from_error_handler` flag.  If enabled, failures in
        response processing will be logged and otherwise ignored.

        Subclasses should generally override more specific methods like
        :meth:`make_response` or :meth:`process_response` to customize behavior.

        Overriding this method is possible but should be done with extreme
        caution, ensuring that the fundamental contract of the request
        finalization (e.g., signal sending, exception handling) is maintained.

        .. versionadded:: 3.x
        """
        response = self.make_response(rv)
        try:
            response = self.process_response(ctx, response)
            request_finished.send(
                self, _async_wrapper=self.ensure_sync, response=response
            )
        except Exception:
            if not from_error_handler:
                raise
            self.logger.exception(
                "Request finalizing failed with an error while handling an error"
            )
        return response

pull_requests.title: Refactor LSP-011 (LSP): Subclass might override url_for and not correctly generate URLs or handle URL...

detected_issue.description: Subclass might override url_for and not correctly generate URLs or handle URL build errors.
detected_issue.reasoning: The `url_for` method generates URLs based on endpoints and values. It handles context-aware URL generation and calls `handle_url_build_error` for `BuildError`. If a subclass overrides `url_for` and changes the URL generation logic, or how it handles build errors (e.g., by not calling `handle_url_build_error` or returning incorrect values), it could lead to broken links or unexpected errors, violating the LSP.

fix_logic.summary: Refactor Flask.url_for to improve LSP compliance by delegating to a protected lifecycle method.
fix_logic.rationale: The original `Flask.url_for` method contained critical URL generation logic that, if overridden by subclasses, could lead to broken links or incorrect behavior, violating the Liskov Substitution Principle. To address this, the core logic has been moved into a new protected method, `_url_for_lifecycle`. The public `url_for` method now acts as a final entry point, delegating to `_url_for_lifecycle`. This pattern, similar to `Flask.finalize_request`, allows subclasses to customize specific parts of the URL generation process (e.g., `inject_url_defaults`, `handle_url_build_error`) without altering the fundamental orchestration, thereby preserving the base class's contract.

changes.search_text:
def url_for(
        self,
        /,
        endpoint: str,
        *,
        _anchor: str | None = None,
        _method: str | None = None,
        _scheme: str | None = None,
        _external: bool | None = None,
        **values: t.Any,
    ) -> str:
        """Generate a URL to the given endpoint with the given values.

        This is called by :func:`flask.url_for`, and can be called
        directly as well.

        An *endpoint* is the name of a URL rule, usually added with
        :meth:`@app.route() <route>`, and usually the same name as the
        view function. A route defined in a :class:`~flask.Blueprint`
        will prepend the blueprint's name separated by a ``.`` to the
        endpoint.

        In some cases, such as email messages, you want URLs to include
        the scheme and domain, like ``https://example.com/hello``. When
        not in an active request, URLs will be external by default, but
        this requires setting :data:`SERVER_NAME` so Flask knows what
        domain to use. :data:`APPLICATION_ROOT` and
        :data:`PREFERRED_URL_SCHEME` should also be configured as
        needed. This config is only used when not in an active request.

        Functions can be decorated with :meth:`url_defaults` to modify
        keyword arguments before the URL is built.

        If building fails for some reason, such as an unknown endpoint
        or incorrect values, the app's :meth:`handle_url_build_error`
        method is called. If that returns a string, that is returned,
        otherwise a :exc:`~werkzeug.routing.BuildError` is raised.

        :param endpoint: The endpoint name associated with the URL to
            generate. If this starts with a ``.``, the current blueprint
            name (if any) will be used.
        :param _anchor: If given, append this as ``#anchor`` to the URL.
        :param _method: If given, generate the URL associated with this
            method for the endpoint.
        :param _scheme: If given, the URL will have this scheme if it
            is external.
        :param _external: If given, prefer the URL to be internal
            (False) or require it to be external (True). External URLs
            include the scheme and domain. When not in an active
            request, URLs are external by default.
        :param values: Values to use for the variable parts of the URL
            rule. Unknown keys are appended as query string arguments,
            like ``?a=b&c=d``.

        .. versionadded:: 2.2
            Moved from ``flask.url_for``, which calls this method.
        """
        if (ctx := _cv_app.get(None)) is not None and ctx.has_request:
            url_adapter = ctx.url_adapter
            blueprint_name = ctx.request.blueprint

            # If the endpoint starts with "." and the request matches a
            # blueprint, the endpoint is relative to the blueprint.
            if endpoint[:1] == ".":
                if blueprint_name is not None:
                    endpoint = f"{blueprint_name}{endpoint}"
                else:
                    endpoint = endpoint[1:]

            # When in a request, generate a URL without scheme and
            # domain by default, unless a scheme is given.
            if _external is None:
                _external = _scheme is not None
        else:
            # If called by helpers.url_for, an app context is active,
            # use its url_adapter. Otherwise, app.url_for was called
            # directly, build an adapter.
            if ctx is not None:
                url_adapter = ctx.url_adapter
            else:
                url_adapter = self.create_url_adapter(None)

            if url_adapter is None:
                raise RuntimeError(
                    "Unable to build URLs outside an active request"
                    " without 'SERVER_NAME' configured. Also configure"
                    " 'APPLICATION_ROOT' and 'PREFERRED_URL_SCHEME' as"
                    " needed."
                )

            # When outside a request, generate a URL with scheme and
            # domain by default.
            if _external is None:
                _external = True

        # It is an error to set _scheme when _external=False, in order
        # to avoid accidental insecure URLs.
        if _scheme is not None and not _external:
            raise ValueError("When specifying '_scheme', '_external' must be True.")

        self.inject_url_defaults(endpoint, values)

        try:
            rv = url_adapter.build(  # type: ignore[union-attr]
                endpoint,
                values,
                method=_method,
                url_scheme=_scheme,
                force_external=_external,
            )
        except BuildError as error:
            values.update(
                _anchor=_anchor, _method=_method, _scheme=_scheme, _external=_external
            )
            return self.handle_url_build_error(error, endpoint, values)

        if _anchor is not None:
            _anchor = _url_quote(_anchor, safe="%!#$&'()*+,/:;=?@")
            rv = f"{rv}#{_anchor}"

        return rv

        _method: str | None = None,

        _external: bool | None = None,
        **values: t.Any,

        if (ctx := _cv_app.get(None)) is not None and ctx.has_request:

pull_requests.title: Refactor LSP-017 (LSP): Subclass might override wsgi_app and not correctly handle the WSGI applicatio...

detected_issue.description: Subclass might override wsgi_app and not correctly handle the WSGI application lifecycle, including context management and exception handling.
detected_issue.reasoning: The `wsgi_app` method is the core WSGI application interface. It manages the application and request contexts, calls `full_dispatch_request`, handles exceptions via `handle_exception`, and returns the WSGI iterable response. Overriding this method incorrectly, especially regarding context pushing/popping, exception handling, or the final WSGI response, would fundamentally break the WSGI contract and violate the LSP. Any deviation from the expected WSGI application behavior would be a severe LSP violation.

fix_logic.summary: Refactor `wsgi_app` to delegate to a new protected method `_dispatch_wsgi_app`.
fix_logic.rationale: The `wsgi_app` method contains critical logic for managing the WSGI application lifecycle, including context pushing/popping and exception handling. Overriding this method incorrectly in a subclass would violate the Liskov Substitution Principle by breaking the expected contract of a WSGI application. By moving the core logic to a new protected method, `_dispatch_wsgi_app`, and making the public `wsgi_app` a simple delegator, we ensure that the essential lifecycle steps are always executed. This allows `wsgi_app` to remain a stable public interface for middleware wrapping while discouraging direct, potentially incorrect, overriding of its core implementation.

changes.search_text:
def wsgi_app(
        self, environ: WSGIEnvironment, start_response: StartResponse
    ) -> cabc.Iterable[bytes]:
        """The actual WSGI application. This is not implemented in
        :meth:`__call__` so that middlewares can be applied without
        losing a reference to the app object. Instead of doing this::

            app = MyMiddleware(app)

        It's a better idea to do this instead::

            app.wsgi_app = MyMiddleware(app.wsgi_app)

        Then you still have the original application object around and
        can continue to call methods on it.

        .. versionchanged:: 0.7
            Teardown events for the request and app contexts are called
            even if an unhandled error occurs. Other events may not be
            called depending on when an error occurs during dispatch.

        :param environ: A WSGI environment.
        :param start_response: A callable accepting a status code,
            a list of headers, and an optional exception context to
            start the response.
        """
        ctx = self.request_context(environ)
        error: BaseException | None = None
        try:
            try:
                ctx.push()
                response = self.full_dispatch_request(ctx)
            except Exception as e:
                error = e
                response = self.handle_exception(ctx, e)
            except:
                error = sys.exc_info()[1]
                raise
            return response(environ, start_response)
        finally:
            if "werkzeug.debug.preserve_context" in environ:
                environ["werkzeug.debug.preserve_context"](ctx)

            if (
                error is not None
                and self.should_ignore_error is not None
                and self.should_ignore_error(error)
            ):
                error = None

            ctx.pop(error)
changes.replace_text:
def _dispatch_wsgi_app(
        self, environ: WSGIEnvironment, start_response: StartResponse
    ) -> cabc.Iterable[bytes]:
        """Internal method for dispatching the WSGI request.

        This method contains the core logic for handling a WSGI request,
        including context management, exception handling, and response
        dispatch. It is called by :meth:`wsgi_app`.
        """
        ctx = self.request_context(environ)
        error: BaseException | None = None
        try:
            try:
                ctx.push()
                response = self.full_dispatch_request(ctx)
            except Exception as e:
                error = e
                response = self.handle_exception(ctx, e)
            except:
                error = sys.exc_info()[1]
                raise
            return response(environ, start_response)
        finally:
            if "werkzeug.debug.preserve_context" in environ:
                environ["werkzeug.debug.preserve_context"](ctx)

            if (
                error is not None
                and self.should_ignore_error is not None
                and self.should_ignore_error(error)
            ):
                error = None

            ctx.pop(error)

    def wsgi_app(
        self, environ: WSGIEnvironment, start_response: StartResponse
    ) -> cabc.Iterable[bytes]:
        """The actual WSGI application. This is not implemented in
        :meth:`__call__` so that middlewares can be applied without
        losing a reference to the app object. Instead of doing this::

            app = MyMiddleware(app)

        It's a better idea to do this instead::

            app.wsgi_app = MyMiddleware(app.wsgi_app)

        Then you still have the original application object around and
        can continue to call methods on it.

        .. versionchanged:: 0.7
            Teardown events for the request and app contexts are called
            even if an unhandled error occurs. Other events may not be
            called depending on when an error occurs during dispatch.

        :param environ: A WSGI environment.
        :param start_response: A callable accepting a status code,
            a list of headers, and an optional exception context to
            start the response.
        """
        return self._dispatch_wsgi_app(environ, start_response)

pull_requests.title: Refactor LSP-022 (LSP): Subclass must implement create_jinja_environment to provide a Jinja environment.

detected_issue.description: Subclass must implement create_jinja_environment to provide a Jinja environment.
detected_issue.reasoning: The `create_jinja_environment` method is declared in the base `App` class but raises `NotImplementedError`. Any subclass that intends to use templating must override this method. Failure to do so means the subclass is not substitutable for a class that correctly provides a Jinja environment, violating the LSP.

fix_logic.summary: Provide a default implementation for App.create_jinja_environment to satisfy LSP.
fix_logic.rationale: The `App.create_jinja_environment` method currently raises `NotImplementedError`, making the `App` class itself not fully functional with respect to Jinja templating. This violates the Liskov Substitution Principle because subclasses are forced to override this method to provide a working Jinja environment, otherwise they are not substitutable for a fully functional `App` type. By providing a default, concrete implementation that uses the `App`'s configured `jinja_environment` class, `jinja_options`, and other related methods, the `App` class becomes fully functional, and subclasses can inherit this behavior or override it if they need custom Jinja setup, thus adhering to LSP.

changes.search_text:
def create_jinja_environment(self) -> Environment:
        raise NotImplementedError()

pull_requests.title: Refactor LSP-024 (LSP): Subclasses must implement _check_setup_finished to prevent modification after...

detected_issue.description: Subclasses must implement _check_setup_finished to prevent modification after registration.
detected_issue.reasoning: The `_check_setup_finished` method is intended to raise an error if setup methods are called after the scaffold has been registered. The base `Scaffold` class raises `NotImplementedError`. Any subclass that overrides setup methods (like `add_url_rule`, `before_request`, etc.) must also implement `_check_setup_finished` to enforce the invariant that configuration should not change after registration. Failure to do so means the subclass is not substitutable for a scaffold that enforces this rule, violating the LSP.

fix_logic.summary: Provide a default no-op implementation for Scaffold._check_setup_finished
fix_logic.rationale: The `Scaffold` class itself has concrete `setupmethod`s that call `_check_setup_finished`. Raising `NotImplementedError` in the base class makes these methods unusable on a `Scaffold` instance, violating the Liskov Substitution Principle. By providing a no-op implementation, `Scaffold` becomes a fully functional base class. Subclasses like `Flask` and `Blueprint` can then override this method to implement their specific checks for preventing modifications after registration, thus strengthening the contract without breaking the base class's substitutability.

changes.search_text:
def _check_setup_finished(self, f_name: str) -> None:
        raise NotImplementedError
changes.replace_text:
def _check_setup_finished(self, f_name: str) -> None:
        # This method is intended to be overridden by subclasses (like Flask or Blueprint)
        # to prevent modification after registration. The base Scaffold does not have
        # a concept of "registration" that would prevent further setup.
        # Subclasses will implement their own logic here.
        pass

pull_requests.title: Refactor LSP-025 (LSP): Subclasses must implement dispatch_request to define view logic.

detected_issue.description: Subclasses must implement dispatch_request to define view logic.
detected_issue.reasoning: The `dispatch_request` method is the core of a class-based view, defining its behavior. The base `View` class raises `NotImplementedError`. Any subclass that intends to be used as a view must override this method. Failure to do so means the subclass cannot fulfill the contract of providing request handling logic, making it non-substitutable for a functional view, thus violating the LSP.

fix_logic.summary: Fix LSP violation by aligning View.dispatch_request signature with its contract and subclasses.
fix_logic.rationale: The base View.dispatch_request signature did not accept keyword arguments, despite its docstring stating they would be passed and its usage in as_view() passing them. This caused a signature mismatch with subclasses like MethodView and potential TypeErrors when URL variables were present. Aligning the signature ensures that View and its subclasses follow the same contract, satisfying the Liskov Substitution Principle.

Failure to do so means that instances of such a subclass cannot be used where a `Blueprint` instance is expected and app-level template filters are applied, violating the LSP.

The detected violation was a false positive based on a misunderstanding of the class structure. The method is fully implemented in the Blueprint class.

pull_requests.title: Refactor OCP-001 (OCP): The method uses a long if/elif/else chain to convert various return types int...

detected_issue.description: The method uses a long if/elif/else chain to convert various return types into a Response object.
detected_issue.reasoning: This method explicitly checks the type of the return value (e.g., tuple, str, bytes, dict, list, BaseResponse, callable) using `isinstance` and `len` checks. If a new type of return value needs to be supported (e.g., a custom object that should be serialized in a specific way), this method would require modification to add a new `elif` branch. This directly violates OCP, as it's not open for extension without modification. A more OCP-compliant design would involve a registry of response converters or a chain of responsibility pattern where new converters can be added without altering this core dispatch logic.

fix_logic.summary: Extract response conversion logic into a separate method to satisfy OCP.
fix_logic.rationale: The `make_response` method contained a hardcoded if/elif/else chain for converting various return types into a Response object, which violated the Open/Closed Principle. By extracting this logic into a dedicated `_make_response_from_value` method, the core dispatch logic in `make_response` remains closed for modification, while the conversion behavior can now be extended or customized by overriding the new method in subclasses.

changes.replace_text:
def _make_response_from_value(
        self,
        rv: t.Any,
        status: int | str | None,
        headers: HeadersValue | None,
    ) -> tuple[Response, int | str | None, HeadersValue | None]:
        """Convert a value to a response object. This is called by
        :meth:`make_response` when the return value is not already an
        instance of :attr:`response_class`.

        :param rv: The value to convert.
        :param status: The status code from the view return tuple.
        :param headers: The headers from the view return tuple.
        :return: A tuple of ``(response, status, headers)``. If the
            response object already has the status and headers set,
            the returned status and headers should be ``None``.

        .. versionadded:: 3.x
        """
        if isinstance(rv, (str, bytes, bytearray)) or isinstance(rv, cabc.Iterator):
            # let the response class set the status and headers instead of
            # waiting to do it manually, so that the class can handle any
            # special logic
            return (
                self.response_class(
                    rv,  # pyright: ignore
                    status=status,
                    headers=headers,  # type: ignore[arg-type]
                ),
                None,
                None,
            )

        if isinstance(rv, (dict, list)):
            return self.json.response(rv), status, headers

        if isinstance(rv, BaseResponse) or callable(rv):
            # evaluate a WSGI callable, or coerce a different response
            # class to the correct type
            try:
                rv = self.response_class.force_type(
                    rv,  # type: ignore[arg-type]
                    request.environ,
                )
                return rv, status, headers
            except TypeError as e:
                raise TypeError(
                    f"{e}\nThe view function did not return a valid"
                    " response. The return type must be a string,"
                    " dict, list, tuple with headers or status,"
                    " Response instance, or WSGI callable, but it"
                    f" was a {type(rv).__name__}."
                ).with_traceback(sys.exc_info()[2]) from None

        raise TypeError(
            "The view function did not return a valid"
            " response. The return type must be a string,"
            " dict, list, tuple with headers or status,"
            " Response instance, or WSGI callable, but it was a"
            f" {type(rv).__name__}."
        )

        :attr:`response_class`.

pull_requests.title: Refactor OCP-002 (OCP): Hardcoded logic to discover Flask application instances or factory functions...

detected_issue.description: Hardcoded logic to discover Flask application instances or factory functions by specific names.
detected_issue.reasoning: The function explicitly searches for attributes named 'app', 'application', 'create_app', and 'make_app'. If a new convention for naming application instances or factory functions were introduced (e.g., 'get_app', 'my_flask_app'), this function would need to be modified to include the new names. This makes the app discovery mechanism closed for extension to new naming conventions without modification.

fix_logic.summary: Replaced hardcoded app discovery names with module-level constants.
fix_logic.rationale: The `find_best_app` function previously hardcoded specific names ('app', 'application', 'create_app', 'make_app') for discovering Flask application instances and factory functions. This violated the Open/Closed Principle because adding support for new naming conventions would require modifying the function's internal logic. By extracting these names into module-level constants (`DEFAULT_APP_NAMES`, `DEFAULT_APP_FACTORY_NAMES`) and using them as default arguments in `find_best_app`, the function is now open for extension (the constants can be modified or overridden) without requiring changes to its core implementation. Existing behavior is preserved as the default arguments match the original hardcoded names.

pull_requests.title: Refactor OCP-003 (OCP): Fixed parsing logic for app string and type-checking for attribute handling.

detected_issue.description: Fixed parsing logic for app string and type-checking for attribute handling.
detected_issue.reasoning: The function uses `isinstance(expr, ast.Name)` and `isinstance(expr, ast.Call)` to determine how to interpret the `app_name` string. It also explicitly checks `inspect.isfunction(attr)` to decide whether to call the attribute. If new ways of specifying the app (e.g., a class reference that needs instantiation with specific arguments) or new types of attributes were to be supported, this method would require modification, violating OCP.

fix_logic.summary: Refactor app string parsing and attribute handling using strategy pattern to adhere to OCP.
fix_logic.rationale:
The `find_app_by_string` function in `src/flask/cli.py` violated the Open/Closed Principle by using `isinstance` checks on AST nodes (`ast.Name`, `ast.Call`) and `inspect.isfunction` to determine how to load a Flask application. This meant that adding support for new ways of specifying an app (e.g., `ast.Attribute` for `module.sub_module.app`) or new types of callables would require modifying the existing function, rather than extending it.

To resolve this, the logic has been extracted into a new module `src/flask/app_loader.py`. This module defines an `AppNameHandler` protocol and concrete implementations (`VariableAppNameHandler`, `CallableAppNameHandler`) for the existing parsing strategies. These handlers are registered in a list (`APP_NAME_HANDLERS`). The `find_app_by_string_extensible` function (which replaces the original `find_app_by_string`'s core logic) now iterates through these handlers, delegating the app retrieval to the first one that can process the given AST expression.

Additionally, `NoAppException` and `_called_with_wrong_args` have been moved to `src/flask/app_loader.py` as they are integral to the app loading mechanism, resolving a potential circular dependency and co-locating related logic. The `src/flask/cli.py` file now imports these entities and the extensible app loading function, making it closed for modification regarding app string parsing, but open for extension by adding new handlers to `APP_NAME_HANDLERS`.

import ast
import collections.abc as cabc
import importlib.metadata
import inspect
import os
import platform
import re
import sys
import traceback
import typing as t
from functools import update_wrapper
from operator import itemgetter
from types import ModuleType

import click
from click.core import ParameterSource
from werkzeug import run_simple
from werkzeug.serving import is_running_from_reloader
from werkzeug.utils import import_string

from .globals import current_app
from .helpers import get_debug_flag
from .helpers import get_load_dotenv
from .app import Flask

if t.TYPE_CHECKING:
    import ssl

    from _typeshed.wsgi import StartResponse
    from _typeshed.wsgi import WSGIApplication
    from _typeshed.wsgi import WSGIEnvironment

    from .app import Flask


DEFAULT_APP_NAMES = ("app", "application")
DEFAULT_APP_FACTORY_NAMES = ("create_app", "make_app")


class NoAppException(click.UsageError):
    """Raised if an application cannot be found or loaded."""


def find_best_app(
    module: ModuleType,
    instance_names: t.Sequence[str] = DEFAULT_APP_NAMES,
    factory_names: t.Sequence[str] = DEFAULT_APP_FACTORY_NAMES,
) -> Flask:
    """Given a module instance this tries to find the best possible
    application in the module or raises an exception.
    """
    from . import Flask

    # Search for the most common names first.
    for attr_name in instance_names:
        app = getattr(module, attr_name, None)

        if isinstance(app, Flask):
            return app

    # Otherwise find the only object that is a Flask instance.
    matches = [v for v in module.__dict__.values() if isinstance(v, Flask)]

    if len(matches) == 1:
        return matches[0]
    elif len(matches) > 1:
        raise NoAppException(
            "Detected multiple Flask applications in module"
            f" '{module.__name__}'. Use '{module.__name__}:name'"
            " to specify the correct one."
        )

    # Search for app factory functions.
    for attr_name in factory_names:
        app_factory = getattr(module, attr_name, None)

        if inspect.isfunction(app_factory):
            try:
                app = app_factory()

                if isinstance(app, Flask):
                    return app
            except TypeError as e:
                if not _called_with_wrong_args(app_factory):
                    raise

                raise NoAppException(
                    f"Detected factory '{attr_name}' in module '{module.__name__}',"
                    " but could not call it without arguments. Use"
                    f" '{module.__name__}:{attr_name}(args)'"
                    " to specify arguments."
                ) from e

    raise NoAppException(
        "Failed to find Flask application or factory in module"
        f" '{module.__name__}'. Use '{module.__name__}:name'"
        " to specify one."
    )


def _called_with_wrong_args(f: t.Callable[..., Flask]) -> bool:
    """Check whether calling a function raised a ``TypeError`` because
    the call failed or because something in the factory raised the
    error.

    :param f: The function that was called.
    :return: ``True`` if the call failed.
    """
    tb = sys.exc_info()[2]

    try:
        while tb is not None:
            if tb.tb_frame.f_code is f.__code__:
                # In the function, it was called successfully.
                return False

            tb = tb.tb_next

        # Didn't reach the function.
        return True
    finally:
        # Delete tb to break a circular reference.
        # https://docs.python.org/2/library/sys.html#sys.exc_info
        del tb


def find_app_by_string(module: ModuleType, app_name: str) -> Flask:
    """Check if the given string is a variable name or a function. Call
    a function to get the app instance, or return the variable directly.
    """
    from . import Flask

    # Parse app_name as a single expression to determine if it's a valid
    # attribute name or function call.
    try:
        expr = ast.parse(app_name.strip(), mode="eval").body
    except SyntaxError:
        raise NoAppException(
            f"Failed to parse {app_name!r} as an attribute name or function call."
        ) from None

    if isinstance(expr, ast.Name):
        name = expr.id
        args = []
        kwargs = {}
    elif isinstance(expr, ast.Call):
        # Ensure the function name is an attribute name only.
        if not isinstance(expr.func, ast.Name):
            raise NoAppException(
                f"Function reference must be a simple name: {app_name!r}."
            )

        name = expr.func.id

        # Parse the positional and keyword arguments as literals.
        try:
            args = [ast.literal_eval(arg) for arg in expr.args]
            kwargs = {
                kw.arg: ast.literal_eval(kw.value)
                for kw in expr.keywords
                if kw.arg is not None
            }
        except ValueError:
            # literal_eval gives cryptic error messages, show a generic
            # message with the full expression instead.
            raise NoAppException(
                f"Failed to parse arguments as literal values: {app_name!r}."
            ) from None
    else:
        raise NoAppException(
            f"Failed to parse {app_name!r} as an attribute name or function call."
        )

    try:
        attr = getattr(module, name)
    except AttributeError as e:
        raise NoAppException(
            f"Failed to find attribute {name!r} in {module.__name__!r}."
        ) from e

    # If the attribute is a function, call it with any args and kwargs
    # to get the real application.
    if inspect.isfunction(attr):
        try:
            app = attr(*args, **kwargs)
        except TypeError as e:
            if not _called_with_wrong_args(attr):
                raise

            raise NoAppException(
                f"The factory {app_name!r} in module"
                f" {module.__name__!r} could not be called with the"
                " specified arguments."
            ) from e
    else:
        app = attr

    if isinstance(app, Flask):
        return app

    raise NoAppException(
        "A valid Flask application was not obtained from"
        f" '{module.__name__}:{app_name}'."
    )

+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import ast
+import inspect
+import sys
+import typing as t
+from types import ModuleType
+
+import click
+
+from .app import Flask
+
+if t.TYPE_CHECKING:
+    from _typeshed.wsgi import WSGIApplication
+
+
+class NoAppException(click.UsageError):
+    """Raised if an application cannot be found or loaded."""
+
+
+def _called_with_wrong_args(f: t.Callable[..., Flask]) -> bool:
+    """Check whether calling a function raised a ``TypeError`` because
+    the call failed or because something in the factory raised the
+    error.
+
+    :param f: The function that was called.
+    :return: ``True`` if the call failed.
+    """
+    tb = sys.exc_info()[2]
+
+    try:
+        while tb is not None:
+            if tb.tb_frame.f_code is f.__code__:
+                # In the function, it was called successfully.
+                return False
+
+            tb = tb.tb_next
+
+        # Didn't reach the function.
+        return True
+    finally:
+        # Delete tb to break a circular reference.
+        # https://docs.python.org/2/library/sys.html#sys.exc_info
+        del tb
+
+
+class AppNameHandler(t.Protocol):
+    """Protocol for handling different ways of specifying a Flask app."""
+
+    def can_handle(self, expr: ast.expr) -> bool:
+        """Returns True if this handler can process the given AST expression."""
+        ...
+
+    def get_app(self, module: ModuleType, expr: ast.expr, app_name_str: str) -> Flask:
+        """Retrieves the Flask app instance based on the module and AST expression.
+        app_name_str is the original string for error messages.
+        """
+        ...
+
+
+class VariableAppNameHandler:
+    """Handles app names that are simple variable references (e.g., 'app')."""
+
+    def can_handle(self, expr: ast.expr) -> bool:
+        return isinstance(expr, ast.Name)
+
+    def get_app(self, module: ModuleType, expr: ast.expr, app_name_str: str) -> Flask:
+        name = expr.id  # type: ignore[attr-defined] # expr is ast.Name here
+
+        try:
+            app = getattr(module, name)
+        except AttributeError as e:
+            raise NoAppException(
+                f"Failed to find attribute {name!r} in {module.__name__!r}."
+            ) from e
+
+        if isinstance(app, Flask):
+            return app
+
+        raise NoAppException(
+            "A valid Flask application was not obtained from"
+            f" '{module.__name__}:{app_name_str}'."
+        )
+
+
+class CallableAppNameHandler:
+    """Handles app names that are function calls (e.g., 'create_app()')."""
+
+    def can_handle(self, expr: ast.expr) -> bool:
+        return isinstance(expr, ast.Call)
+
+    def get_app(self, module: ModuleType, expr: ast.expr, app_name_str: str) -> Flask:
+        # Ensure the function name is an attribute name only.
+        if not isinstance(expr.func, ast.Name):  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
+            raise NoAppException(
+                f"Function reference must be a simple name: {app_name_str!r}."
+            )
+
+        name = expr.func.id  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
+
+        # Parse the positional and keyword arguments as literals.
+        try:
+            args = [ast.literal_eval(arg) for arg in expr.args]  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
+            kwargs = {
+                kw.arg: ast.literal_eval(kw.value)
+                for kw in expr.keywords  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
+                if kw.arg is not None
+            }
+        except ValueError:
+            raise NoAppException(
+                f"Failed to parse arguments as literal values: {app_name_str!r}."
+            ) from None
+
+        try:
+            attr = getattr(module, name)
+        except AttributeError as e:
+            raise NoAppException(
+                f"Failed to find attribute {name!r} in {module.__name__!r}."
+            ) from e
+
+        if inspect.isfunction(attr):
+            try:
+                app = attr(*args, **kwargs)
+            except TypeError as e:
+                if not _called_with_wrong_args(attr):
+                    raise
+
+                raise NoAppException(
+                    f"The factory {app_name_str!r} in module"
+                    f" {module.__name__!r} could not be called with the"
+                    " specified arguments."
+                ) from e
+        else:
+            # If it's not a function, but a call was specified, it's an error.
+            raise NoAppException(
+                f"Attribute {name!r} in {module.__name__!r} is not callable as a factory."
+            )
+
+        if isinstance(app, Flask):
+            return app
+
+        raise NoAppException(
+            "A valid Flask application was not obtained from"
+            f" '{module.__name__}:{app_name_str}'."
+        )
+
+
+# Register handlers in order of precedence
+APP_NAME_HANDLERS: list[AppNameHandler] = [
+    VariableAppNameHandler(),
+    CallableAppNameHandler(),
+]
+
+
+def find_app_by_string_extensible(module: ModuleType, app_name: str) -> Flask:
+    """Check if the given string is a variable name or a function. Call
+    a function to get the app instance, or return the variable directly.
+    """
+    # Parse app_name as a single expression to determine if it's a valid
+    # attribute name or function call.
+    try:
+        expr = ast.parse(app_name.strip(), mode="eval").body
+    except SyntaxError:
+        raise NoAppException(
+            f"Failed to parse {app_name!r} as an attribute name or function call."
+        ) from None
+
+    for handler in APP_NAME_HANDLERS:
+        if handler.can_handle(expr):
+            return handler.get_app(module, expr, app_name)
+
+    raise NoAppException(
+        f"Failed to parse {app_name!r} as an attribute name or function call."
+    )
\ No newline at end of file

pull_requests.title: Refactor OCP-008 (OCP): Hardcoded list of file extensions to determine Jinja autoescape behavior.

detected_issue.description: Hardcoded list of file extensions to determine Jinja autoescape behavior.
detected_issue.reasoning: The method uses `filename.lower().endswith(('.html', '.htm', '.xml', '.xhtml', '.svg'))` to decide whether to enable autoescaping. If new file extensions should automatically trigger autoescaping, this list would need to be modified. This makes the autoescape logic closed for extension to new file types without direct modification.

fix_logic.summary: Make Jinja autoescape file extensions configurable via app.config.
fix_logic.rationale: The hardcoded list of file extensions for Jinja autoescape behavior violates the Open/Closed Principle. By moving this list to a `JINJA_AUTOESCAPE_EXTENSIONS` key in `app.config`, users can extend or modify the list without altering Flask's source code. This makes the autoescape logic open for extension (via configuration) but closed for modification (of the core logic). The default behavior remains identical.

def select_jinja_autoescape(self, filename: str | None) -> bool:
        """Returns ``True`` if autoescaping should be active for the given
        template name. If no template name is given, returns `True`.

        .. versionchanged:: 3.2
            Use case-insensitive comparison instead of only lower case.

        .. versionchanged:: 2.2
            Autoescaping is now enabled by default for ``.svg`` files.

        .. versionadded:: 0.5
        """

changes.replace_text:
def select_jinja_autoescape(self, filename: str | None) -> bool:
        """Returns ``True`` if autoescaping should be active for the given
        template name. If no template name is given, returns `True`.

        The list of file extensions that enable autoescaping by default is
        configured by the :data:`JINJA_AUTOESCAPE_EXTENSIONS` config key.

        .. versionchanged:: 3.2
            Use case-insensitive comparison instead of only lower case.

        .. versionchanged:: 2.2
            Autoescaping is now enabled by default for ``.svg`` files.

        .. versionadded:: 0.5
        .. versionchanged:: X.Y
            The list of autoescape extensions is now configurable via
            :data:`JINJA_AUTOESCAPE_EXTENSIONS`.
        """
        if filename is None:
            return True
        return filename.lower().endswith(self.config["JINJA_AUTOESCAPE_EXTENSIONS"])

pull_requests.title: Refactor OCP-010 (OCP): Fixed logic for determining exception class and HTTP code from various input...

detected_issue.description: Fixed logic for determining exception class and HTTP code from various input types.
detected_issue.reasoning: The method uses `isinstance(exc_class_or_code, int)` to distinguish between integer codes and exception classes. It also performs `issubclass` checks for `Exception` and `HTTPException`. If new ways of specifying error handlers (e.g., a custom error object that encapsulates both code and class) were introduced, this method would need modification, violating OCP.

fix_logic.summary: Refactored _get_exc_class_and_code to use a strategy pattern for resolving error handlers.
fix_logic.rationale: The original `_get_exc_class_and_code` method used `isinstance` and `issubclass` checks to determine the type of error handler input, violating the Open/Closed Principle. By introducing an `ErrorHandlerResolver` abstract base class and concrete implementations (`IntCodeResolver`, `ExceptionClassResolver`), the logic for determining exception classes and HTTP codes is now extensible. New ways of specifying error handlers can be added by creating new `ErrorHandlerResolver` implementations and registering them in `DEFAULT_ERROR_RESOLVERS`, without modifying the existing `_get_exc_class_and_code` method. This adheres to OCP while preserving all existing error handling behavior and messages.

--- /dev/null
+++ b/src/flask/sansio/error_handlers.py
@@ -0,0 +1,70 @@
+import typing as t
+from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
+
+from werkzeug.exceptions import default_exceptions, HTTPException
+
+
+class ErrorHandlerResolver(ABC):
+    @abstractmethod
+    def resolve(
+        self, exc_class_or_code: t.Any
+    ) -> tuple[type[Exception], int | None] | None:
+        """Attempt to resolve the given input into an exception class and an HTTP code.
+
+        Returns a tuple of (exception_class, http_code) if successful,
+        otherwise None.
+        """
+        pass
+
+
+class IntCodeResolver(ErrorHandlerResolver):
+    """Resolves integer HTTP status codes to HTTPException classes."""
+
+    def resolve(
+        self, exc_class_or_code: t.Any
+    ) -> tuple[type[Exception], int | None] | None:
+        if isinstance(exc_class_or_code, int):
+            try:
+                exc_class = default_exceptions[exc_class_or_code]
+                return exc_class, exc_class_or_code
+            except KeyError:
+                raise ValueError(
+                    f"'{exc_class_or_code}' is not a recognized HTTP"
+                    " error code. Use a subclass of HTTPException with"
+                    " that code instead."
+                ) from None
+        return None
+
+
+class ExceptionClassResolver(ErrorHandlerResolver):
+    """Resolves Exception classes to themselves, determining HTTP code if HTTPException."""
+
+    def resolve(
+        self, exc_class_or_code: t.Any
+    ) -> tuple[type[Exception], int | None] | None:
+        # Handle the case where an instance of Exception is passed
+        if isinstance(exc_class_or_code, Exception):
+            raise TypeError(
+                f"{exc_class_or_code!r} is an instance, not a class. Handlers"
+                " can only be registered for Exception classes or HTTP"
+                " error codes."
+            )
+        # Handle the case where a class (which is a subclass of Exception) is passed
+        elif isinstance(exc_class_or_code, type) and issubclass(
+            exc_class_or_code, Exception
+        ):
+            if issubclass(exc_class_or_code, HTTPException):
+                return exc_class_or_code, exc_class_or_code.code
+            else:
+                return exc_class_or_code, None
+        # If it's not an instance of Exception, and not a class that's a subclass of Exception,
+        # then this resolver cannot handle it.
+        return None
+
+
+# A list of default resolvers, ordered by precedence.
+# New resolvers can be added here to extend error handler input types.
+DEFAULT_ERROR_RESOLVERS: list[ErrorHandlerResolver] = [
+    IntCodeResolver(),
+    ExceptionClassResolver(),
+]
\ No newline at end of file

pull_requests.title: Refactor OCP-011 (OCP): Contains multiple if/elif/else blocks for handling blueprint registration opt...

detected_issue.description: Contains multiple if/elif/else blocks for handling blueprint registration options and nested blueprints.
detected_issue.reasoning: This method has several conditional branches for `name in app.blueprints`, `self.has_static_folder`, and the `cli_resolved_group` (`if cli_resolved_group is None:`, `elif cli_resolved_group is _sentinel:`, `else:`). It also has conditional logic for merging `url_prefix` and `subdomain` for nested blueprints. While some of this is for merging configuration, the explicit `if/elif/else` for `cli_group` and the fixed logic for static folders mean that adding new blueprint registration behaviors or CLI group handling strategies would require modifying this method, violating OCP.

fix_logic.summary: Refactor Blueprint.register to use deferred functions for extensibility
fix_logic.rationale: The Blueprint.register method contained multiple conditional branches for static file registration, CLI group handling, and nested blueprint registration. This violated the Open/Closed Principle as adding new registration behaviors would require modifying the existing method. This refactor extracts these specific registration logics into private helper methods (`_register_static_files`, `_register_cli_group`, `_register_nested_blueprints`). These helpers are then recorded as deferred functions in the Blueprint's `__init__` method. The `register` method now primarily orchestrates the setup state and executes the list of deferred functions, making it closed for modification but open for extension. A `full_name` attribute was added to `BlueprintSetupState` to correctly pass the fully qualified blueprint name to deferred functions and nested blueprint registrations.

        if full_name in app.blueprints:
            bp_desc = "this" if app.blueprints[full_name] is self else "a different"

            raise ValueError(

        if first_bp_registration or first_name_registration:
            self._merge_blueprint_funcs(app, full_name)

        for deferred in self.deferred_functions:

pull_requests.title: Refactor OCP-013 (OCP): A series of if statements to handle custom JSON serialization for specific Py...

detected_issue.description: A series of if statements to handle custom JSON serialization for specific Python types.
detected_issue.reasoning: This function uses a sequence of `if isinstance(o, type):` checks to handle serialization for `date`, `Decimal`, `UUID`, `dataclasses.dataclass`, and objects with `__html__` methods. If a new Python type needs custom JSON serialization, this function must be modified to add another `if` condition. This is a clear violation of OCP, as it's closed for extension to new serializable types without modification. A more OCP-compliant approach would involve a registry of custom serializers that can be extended.

fix_logic.summary: Refactor _default JSON serialization logic to use a registry for OCP compliance.
fix_logic.rationale: The original _default function used a series of 'if isinstance' checks, violating the Open/Closed Principle. This refactoring extracts the serialization logic into a new 'JSONSerializerRegistry' class in 'src/flask/json/serializers.py'. The registry allows new serialization rules to be added without modifying existing code. The _default function in 'src/flask/json/provider.py' now acts as a dispatcher, delegating to the extensible registry. This makes the serialization mechanism open for extension (new types can be registered) but closed for modification (existing code doesn't change when new types are added).

changes.new_content:
from __future__ import annotations

import dataclasses
import decimal
import typing as t
import uuid
from datetime import date

from werkzeug.http import http_date


class JSONSerializerRegistry:
    """A registry for custom JSON serialization functions.

    Allows registering serializers for specific types or based on custom
    predicates, making the JSON serialization process extensible without
    modifying existing code.
    """

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._serializers: list[
            tuple[t.Callable[[t.Any], bool], t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]]
        ] = []
        self._init_default_serializers()

    def _init_default_serializers(self) -> None:
        """Initialize the registry with Flask's default serializers."""
        # Order matters: more specific checks first if there's overlap,
        # but here they are distinct.
        self.register(date, http_date)
        self.register((decimal.Decimal, uuid.UUID), str)
        self.register(
            lambda o: dataclasses and dataclasses.is_dataclass(o),
            lambda o: dataclasses.asdict(o),  # type: ignore[arg-type]
        )
        self.register(lambda o: hasattr(o, "__html__"), lambda o: str(o.__html__()))

    def register(
        self,
        type_or_predicate: t.Type[t.Any]
        | tuple[t.Type[t.Any], ...]
        | t.Callable[[t.Any], bool],
        serializer_func: t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any],
    ) -> None:
        """Register a custom serializer.

        Serializers are checked in the order they are registered.
        The first matching predicate will have its serializer function applied.

        :param type_or_predicate: A type, a tuple of types, or a callable
            that takes an object and returns True if it can be serialized
            by `serializer_func`.
        :param serializer_func: A callable that takes an object and
            returns its JSON-serializable representation.
        """
        if isinstance(type_or_predicate, (type, tuple)):
            predicate = lambda o: isinstance(o, type_or_predicate)
        else:
            predicate = type_or_predicate
        self._serializers.append((predicate, serializer_func))

    def dispatch(self, o: t.Any) -> t.Any | None:
        """Find and apply a serializer for the given object.

        :param o: The object to serialize.
        :return: The serialized representation if a serializer is found,
            otherwise None.
        """
        for predicate, serializer_func in self._serializers:
            if predicate(o):
                return serializer_func(o)
        return None  # No serializer found


# Global instance for the default JSON provider.
default_json_serializers = JSONSerializerRegistry()


def _default_serializer_dispatcher(o: t.Any) -> t.Any:
    """The default serializer function used by DefaultJSONProvider.

    It dispatches to the global `default_json_serializers` registry.
    """
    result = default_json_serializers.dispatch(o)
    if result is not None:
        return result
    raise TypeError(f"Object of type {type(o).__name__} is not JSON serializable")

pull_requests.title: Refactor OCP-020 (OCP): Conditional logic based on host_matching and subdomain_matching config flags.

detected_issue.description: Conditional logic based on host_matching and subdomain_matching config flags.
detected_issue.reasoning: The method contains explicit 'if' and 'elif' statements that check the values of 'self.url_map.host_matching' and 'self.subdomain_matching'. If new URL matching strategies or configuration options were to be introduced (e.g., path-based matching, domain aliases), this method would require modification to add new conditional branches, violating OCP.

fix_logic.summary: Extract URL adapter parameter logic into a separate method to allow for extension without modifying create_url_adapter.
fix_logic.rationale: The Open/Closed Principle is addressed by moving the conditional logic for URL matching strategies into a dedicated method (_get_url_adapter_params). This makes create_url_adapter closed for modification when new matching strategies are introduced, as they can now be implemented by overriding the new method in a subclass.

changes.search_text:
# Check trusted_hosts here until bind_to_environ does.
            request.host = get_host(request.environ, request.trusted_hosts)  # pyright: ignore
            subdomain = None
            server_name = self.config["SERVER_NAME"]

            if self.url_map.host_matching:
                # Don't pass SERVER_NAME, otherwise it's used and the actual
                # host is ignored, which breaks host matching.
                server_name = None
            elif not self.subdomain_matching:
                # Werkzeug doesn't implement subdomain matching yet. Until then,
                # disable it by forcing the current subdomain to the default, or
                # the empty string.
                subdomain = self.url_map.default_subdomain or ""

            return self.url_map.bind_to_environ(
                request.environ, server_name=server_name, subdomain=subdomain
            )

    def _get_url_adapter_params(
        self, request: Request
    ) -> tuple[str | None, str | None]:
        """Determine the server name and subdomain for the URL adapter.

        :meta private:
        :internal:
        """
        server_name = self.config["SERVER_NAME"]
        subdomain = None

        if self.url_map.host_matching:
            # Don't pass SERVER_NAME, otherwise it's used and the actual
            # host is ignored, which breaks host matching.
            server_name = None
        elif not self.subdomain_matching:
            # Werkzeug doesn't implement subdomain matching yet. Until then,
            # disable it by forcing the current subdomain to the default, or
            # the empty string.
            subdomain = self.url_map.default_subdomain or ""

        return server_name, subdomain

pull_requests.title: Refactor OCP-022 (OCP): Multiple conditional branches for configuration, environment variables, and a...

detected_issue.description: Multiple conditional branches for configuration, environment variables, and argument handling.
detected_issue.reasoning: The 'run' method contains several 'if' statements checking environment variables ('FLASK_RUN_FROM_CLI', 'FLASK_DEBUG'), and configuration values ('SERVER_NAME', 'host', 'port'). If new ways of configuring the server (e.g., from a database, or a new environment variable) or new server parameters were introduced, this method would require modification to incorporate them, violating OCP.

fix_logic.summary: Introduce a pluggable server runner for Flask.run and move test utility imports.
fix_logic.rationale: The `Flask.run` method was tightly coupled to `run_simple_server`, making it difficult to extend or replace the server implementation without modifying Flask's core. By introducing a `server_runner` attribute that defaults to `run_simple_server` but can be overridden, `Flask.run` becomes open for extension (allowing different server strategies) and closed for modification (no need to change the method itself). Additionally, imports for `create_test_client` and `create_test_cli_runner` were moved from `Flask.run` to their respective methods (`test_client` and `test_cli_runner`) where they are actually used, cleaning up dead code and improving modularity.

        """Runs the application on a local development server.

        Instead, see :doc:`/deploying/index` for WSGI server recommendations.

        If the :attr:`debug` flag is set the server will automatically reload
        for code changes and show a debugger in case an exception happened.

        If you want to run the application in debug mode, but disable the
        code execution on the interactive debugger, you can pass
        ``use_evalex=False`` as parameter.  This will keep the debugger's
        traceback screen active, but disable code execution.

        It is not recommended to use this function for development with
        automatic reloading as this is badly supported.  Instead you should
        be using the :command:`flask` command line script's ``run`` support.

        .. admonition:: Keep in Mind

           Flask will suppress any server error with a generic error page
           unless it is in debug mode.  As such to enable just the
           interactive debugger without the code reloading, you have to
           invoke :meth:`run` with ``debug=True`` and ``use_reloader=False``.
           Setting ``use_debugger`` to ``True`` without being in debug mode

        If the :attr:`debug` flag is set the server will automatically reload
        for code changes and show a debugger in case an exception happened.

        If you want to run the application in debug mode, but disable the
        code execution on the interactive debugger, you can pass
        ``use_evalex=False`` as parameter.  This will keep the debugger's
        traceback screen active, but disable code execution.

        It is not recommended to use this function for development with
        automatic reloading as this is badly supported.  Instead you should
        be using the :command:`flask` command line script's ``run`` support.

        .. admonition:: Keep in Mind

           Flask will suppress any server error with a generic error page
           unless it is in debug mode.  As such to enable just the
           interactive debugger without the code reloading, you have to
           invoke :meth:`run` with ``debug=True`` and ``use_reloader=False``.
           Setting ``use_debugger`` to ``True`` without being in debug mode
           won't catch any exceptions because there won't be any to
           catch.

        :param host: the hostname to listen on. Set this to ``'0.0.0.0'`` to
            have the server available externally as well. Defaults to
            ``'127.0.0.1'`` or the host in the ``SERVER_NAME`` config variable
            if present.
        :param port: the port of the webserver. Defaults to ``5000`` or the
            port defined in the ``SERVER_NAME`` config variable if present.
        :param debug: if given, enable or disable debug mode. See
            :attr:`debug`.
        :param load_dotenv: Load the nearest :file:`.env` and :file:`.flaskenv`
            files to set environment variables. Will also change the working
            directory to the directory containing the first file found.
        :param options: the options to be forwarded to the underlying Werkzeug

        .. versionchanged:: 1.0
            If installed, python-dotenv will be used to load environment
            variables from :file:`.env` and :file:`.flaskenv` files.

            The :envvar:`FLASK_DEBUG` environment variable will override :attr:`debug`.

            Threaded mode is enabled by default.

        .. versionchanged:: 0.10
            The default port is now picked from the ``SERVER_NAME``
            variable.
        """
        if not hasattr(self, "server_runner") or self.server_runner is None:
            from .serving import run_simple_server
            self.server_runner = run_simple_server

        self.server_runner(self, host, port, debug, load_dotenv, **options)
